Note also that eleasticsearch-py by default doesn't verify certificates, which is pretty insecure:
https://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/#ssl-and-authentication

Options:
1. continue to use the (default) elasticsearch-py urllib3 backend, but install certifi and then pass `verify_certs=True` and `ca_certs=certifi.where()` iff `'https' in settings.ELASTIC_SEARCH["url"]` (Note we're trying to move away from environment-specific variables like `IS_HEROKU`). See docs: https://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/#ssl-and-authentication
2. use the elasticsearch-py requests backend, and just pass `verify_certs=True`, which should then use requests' vendored version of certifi automatically

Fwiw it seems the default urllib3 backend is preferred:
https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/issues/402

I've also filed an issue against elasticsearch-py, to reduce the boilerplate for option (1) above:
https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/issues/403

Also depending on how that last filed issue goes, ideally I'd get them to default to `verify_certs=True`, the current default is such a footgun.

Well, though I in general agree that turning caching on is a sane default in general, I do disagree in this particular case. The discovery caching in `googleapiclient` is mostly broken, as indicated in the linked issues, and nobody tried to fix this upstream for several years. The chances of file caching working are even more reduced by #2361 dropping `oauth2client` dependency.
The best solution would be to have some kind of simple in-memory cache without external dependencies by default in the upstream `googleapiclient`, but I do not believe this is a Luigi issue.
What Luigi can do, though, is to ensure GCP-related functionality works out of the box, without getting scary `ImportError` log spam or learning about API discovery caching (which is not that important to know about).
So I would say that getting a way to pass custom discovery kwargs is certainly an improvement, but I feel that Luigi should change the default of the `cache_discovery` value, at least until the discovery caching works out of the box in the upstream library. It is a simple matter of doing `kwargs.setdefault('cache_discovery', False)`.

- Better not to mention anything about arguments if there are none. Current `""` looks odd.

This patching was pretty complicated beforehand - it was relying on some very specific implementation details of the requests library to simulate fake response objects.  It was also hard to mock properly because now there's two requests being made (one to determine the API version, and the other for `/api/v1/ping/`.

So I've simplified this test to really just perform the API version detection request so that we can make sure that `verify=False` was used properly (which is the whole point of this test).

In addition, `children` use to always be a `list` with the old `dict` implementation but now it might be `None`. Since in some places the code does `elt.children.append(child)`, if `children` is `None`, it will crash.

Proposed implementation:
```python
def __init__(self, type_, attr=None, children=None, markup=None):
    self.type = type_
    self.attr = attr
    self.children = children if children is not None else []
    self.markup = markup
```

We also already have a temporary file context manager that attempts to handle deletions on Windows. Additionally, pytest has a type of fixture called "yield fixture" that is their version of a fixture that requires a tearDown step.

So, your `temp_output` instance variable would be written like this in pytest:



```python
import pytest
from odo.utils import tmpfile


@pytest.yield_fixture
def temp_output():
    with tmpfile('.avro') as fn:
        with open(fn, 'w+b') as f:
            yield f
```

On my machine, /etc/mime.types maps `asc` to `application/pgp-signature`. As a consequence, we currently don't gzip it. For example:
```sh
 curl -I https://public-artifacts.taskcluster.net/SwE-nbJ_RQalWmmEfMbCuA/0/public/chainOfTrust.json.asc
```

I agree this may not be the case on every machine. I see several solutions:

* Either we don't gzip `asc` file. In this case, we have to:
  * document `/etc/mime.types/` is extensively used, and different machines may provide different uploads
  * make the puppet module fill `/etc/mime.types` for us. For the record, our CentOS VMs don't have this file.
* Or we gzip files that are listed (hardcoded?) in scriptworker. At the moment, I'm thinking of files like: logs, json, asc, html/xml. If we go this way, here are the implications:
  * **Browsers** will be able to gunzip *some files*, which means:
    * If you want to use curl or wget, you have to pass extra args or pipe them (respectively `--compressed` and `wget ... | gunzip -c -`). Documenting that behavior would be nice.
    * If you want browser to understand every file, you have to force `Content-Type` to `text/plain`. This doesn't cause any issues once the file is downloaded. 

I checkout out some existing jobs listed on Treeherder. The majority of them just gzip live_backing.log. However, I saw some of them compressing html or json files.

I currently prefer the second solution, for 4 reasons:

1. We save space (and bandwidth) on S3, so the cost of hosting artifacts is lower.
2. Less unexpected behaviors to document. 
3. Every text file is handled the same.
4. Thanks to specifying `text/plain` for artifacts like `asc`, we make them readable from the browser (instead of downloadable, which happens on my computer). Thus, less steps to see the content of these files, faster dev/troubleshooting!
